code: @Manual{,   title = {arg: Clean, Simple Argument Checking},   author = {Noah Greifer},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://ngreifer.github.io/arg/}, }

title: Clean, Simple Argument Checking
text: arg produces nice, clean error messages checking function arguments R packages. developed arg often found writing error messages packages, wasn’t satisfied similar R packages available. arg uses cli produce clean clear errors without requiring much programming developer. messages designed clear user, using complicated language. Many messages adapt type (incorrect) input received. name “arg” meant short “argument”, also perhaps represents sound make encounter error running code. Hopefully arg reduces number times users say “arg”. use R packages, including MatchIt, WeightIt, cobalt, optweight, adrftools, may already using arg!
code: library(arg)  z <- "a string"  arg_string(z)  arg_number(z) #> Error: #> ! `z` must be a number.  arg_and(z,         arg_string,         arg_length(2L)) #> Error: #> ! All of the following conditions must be met: #> ✔ `z` must be a string #> ✖ `z` must have length 2  arg_or(z,        arg_is_NA,        arg_flag,        arg_count) #> Error: #> ! `z` must be NA, a logical value (TRUE or FALSE), or a count (a #>   non-negative whole number).

what: arg: Clean, Simple Argument Checking — arg-package

title: arg: Clean, Simple Argument Checking — arg-package
text: Maintainer: Noah Greifer noah.greifer@gmail.com (ORCID)

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
text: Checks whether values within range (arg_between()), greater value (arg_gt()), greater equal value (arg_gte()), less value (arg_lt()), less equal value (arg_lte()).

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
code: arg_between(   x,   range = c(0, 1),   inclusive = TRUE,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .msg = NULL )  arg_gt(x, bound = 0, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_gte(x, bound = 0, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_lt(x, bound = 0, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_lte(x, bound = 0, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
text: x argument checked range vector two values identifying required range. inclusive logical vector identifying whether range boundaries inclusive . one value supplied, applied range boundaries. Default TRUE, throw error x equal boundaries. .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message. bound bound check . Default 0.

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
text: x checked type, possible values numeric values passed compared; however, error thrown typeof(x) equal typeof(range) typeof(bound). NA values x removed. arguments range, inclusive, bound checked appropriateness.

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
code: z <- 2  try(arg_between(z, c(1, 3))) # No error try(arg_between(z, c(1, 2))) # No error try(arg_between(z, c(1, 2),                 inclusive = FALSE)) # Error #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `z` must be between 1 and 2 (exclusive). try(arg_between(z, c(1, 2),                 inclusive = c(TRUE, FALSE))) # Error #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `z` must be greater than or equal to 1 and less than 2.  try(arg_gt(z, 0))  # No error try(arg_gt(z, 2))  # Error #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `z` must be greater than 2. try(arg_gte(z, 2)) # No error  try(arg_lt(z, 0))  # Error #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `z` must be negative. try(arg_lt(z, 2))  # Error #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `z` must be less than 2. try(arg_lte(z, 2)) # No error  try(arg_lte(z, "3")) # Error: wrong type #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `z` must be less than or equal to "3".

title: Check Character Argument — arg_character
text: Checks whether argument character vector (arg_character()), character scalar (arg_string()), factor (arg_factor()).

title: Check Character Argument — arg_character
code: arg_character(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_string(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_factor(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Character Argument — arg_character
text: x argument checked .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Character Argument — arg_character
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Character Argument — arg_character
text: NA values arg_string() cause error thrown.

title: Check Character Argument — arg_character
code: f <- function(z) {   arg_string(z) }  try(f("a")) # No error try(f(c("a", "b"))) # Error: arg_string() requires scalar #> Error in f(c("a", "b")) : `z` must be a string. try(f(NA)) # NAs not allowed for arg_string() #> Error in f(NA) : `z` must be a string.

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
text: Checks whether argument atomic vector (arg_atomic()), dimensionless atomic vector (arg_vector()), list (arg_list()), data frame (arg_data.frame()), matrix (arg_matrix()), array (arg_array()), rectangular data set (arg_data()), environment (arg_env()).

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
code: arg_atomic(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_vector(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_list(x, df_ok = FALSE, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_data.frame(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_matrix(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_array(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_data(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_env(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
text: x argument checked .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message. df_ok logical; whether allow data frames (technically lists). Default FALSE throw error data frame input.

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
text: Atomic vectors checked using .atomic(). matrices considered atomic vectors, arg_vector() additionally checks "dims" attribute, .e., length(dim(x)) == 0L. arg_data() checks whether x either data frame matrix. arg_list() checks whether x list; df = FALSE, throws error x data frame, even though data frames lists.

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
code: vec <- 1:6 mat <- as.matrix(vec) dat <- as.data.frame(mat) lis <- as.list(vec) nul <- NULL  # arg_atomic() try(arg_atomic(vec)) try(arg_atomic(mat)) try(arg_atomic(dat)) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `dat` must be an atomic vector. try(arg_atomic(lis)) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `lis` must be an atomic vector. try(arg_atomic(nul)) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `nul` must be an atomic vector.  # arg_vector() try(arg_vector(vec)) try(arg_vector(mat)) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `mat` must be a vector.  # arg_matrix() try(arg_matrix(vec)) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `vec` must be a matrix. try(arg_matrix(mat)) try(arg_matrix(dat)) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `dat` must be a matrix.  # arg_data.frame() try(arg_data.frame(vec)) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `vec` must be a data frame. try(arg_data.frame(mat)) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `mat` must be a data frame. try(arg_data.frame(dat))  # arg_data() try(arg_data(vec)) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `vec` must be a data frame or matrix. try(arg_data(mat)) try(arg_data(dat))

title: Check Supplied Dots Argument — arg_dots_supplied
text: Checks whether argument supplied ....

title: Check Supplied Dots Argument — arg_dots_supplied
code: arg_dots_supplied(..., .msg = NULL)  arg_dots_not_supplied(..., .msg = NULL)

title: Check Supplied Dots Argument — arg_dots_supplied
text: ... ... argument passed calling function. argument evaluated. See Examples. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Supplied Dots Argument — arg_dots_supplied
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Supplied Dots Argument — arg_dots_supplied
text: arg_dots_supplied() checks whether arguments passed ... (.e., "dots") argument function. arguments evaluated. arg_dots_supplied() throws error ...length() 0. can useful function must additional arguments. example, arg_or() when_supplied() use arg_dots_supplied(). arg_dots_not_supplied() checks whether arguments passed ..., without evaluating arguments using ...length(). can useful function appears allow arguments (e.g., method generic), author want user supply .

title: Check Supplied Dots Argument — arg_dots_supplied
code: f <- function(...) {   arg_dots_supplied(...) }  try(f(1)) # No error: argument supplied try(f())  # Error! #> Error in f() : An argument must be supplied to `...`.

title: Check Element — arg_element
text: Checks whether values argument elements set (arg_element()) elements set (arg_not_element()). arg_element() throws error (.element(x, values)) FALSE, arg_not_element() throws error (.element(x, values)) TRUE.

title: Check Element — arg_element
code: arg_element(x, values, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_not_element(x, values, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Element — arg_element
text: x argument checked values values check x . .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Element — arg_element
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Element — arg_element
text: arg_element() can useful checking whether argument matches one allowed values. important check done beforehand ensure x correct type, e.g., using arg_string() arg_character() values character vector. partial matching used; values must match exactly. Use match_arg() allow partial matching return full version supplied argument. arg_not_element() can useful ensuring duplication values argument set.

title: Check Element — arg_element
code: f <- function(z) {   arg_element(z, c("opt1", "opt2", "opt3")) }  try(f("opt1"))            # No error try(f(c("opt1", "opt2"))) # No error, all are elements try(f(c("opt1", "opt1"))) # No error: repeats allowed try(f("bad_arg"))         # Error: not an element of set #> Error in f("bad_arg") :  #>   `z` must be one of "opt1", "opt2", or "opt3". try(f("opt"))             # Error: partial matching not allowed #> Error in f("opt") :  #>   `z` must be one of "opt1", "opt2", or "opt3". try(f(c("opt1", "bad_arg"))) # Error: one non-match #> Error in f(c("opt1", "bad_arg")) :  #>   Each element of `z` must be one of "opt1", "opt2", or "opt3".  g <- function(z) {   arg_not_element(z, c("bad1", "bad2", "bad3")) }  try(g("bad1"))            # Error: z is an element #> Error in g("bad1") :  #>   `z` must not be one of "bad1", "bad2", or "bad3". try(g(c("bad1", "opt2"))) # Error: at least one bad match #> Error in g(c("bad1", "opt2")) :  #>   No element of `z` may be one of "bad1", "bad2", or "bad3". try(g("opt1"))            # No error: not an element try(g(c("opt1", "opt2"))) # No error, none are elements

title: Check Equal Arguments — arg_equal
text: Checks whether two arguments equal (arg_equal()) equal (arg_not_equal()).

title: Check Equal Arguments — arg_equal
code: arg_equal(   x,   x2,   ...,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .arg2 = rlang::caller_arg(x2),   .msg = NULL )  arg_not_equal(   x,   x2,   ...,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .arg2 = rlang::caller_arg(x2),   .msg = NULL )

title: Check Equal Arguments — arg_equal
text: x, x2 objects compare . ... arguments passed .equal(). .arg, .arg2 names objects compared; used error message triggered. Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Equal Arguments — arg_equal
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Equal Arguments — arg_equal
text: Tests equality performed evaluating whether isTRUE(.equal(x, x2, ...)) TRUE FALSE.

title: Check Equal Arguments — arg_equal
code: f <- function(x, y, ...) {   arg_equal(x, y, ...) }  try(f(x = 1, y = 1))      ## No error try(f(x = 1L, y = 1.0))   ## No error despite type difference try(f(x = 1, y = 1.00001, ## No error, within tolerance       tolerance = .001))  try(f(x = 1, y = 2))       ## Error: different #> Error in f(x = 1, y = 2) : `x` must be equal to `y`. try(f(x = 1, y = 1.00001)) ## Error #> Error in f(x = 1, y = 1.00001) : `x` must be equal to `y`.  g <- function(x, y, ...) {   arg_not_equal(x, y, ...) }  try(g(x = 1, y = 1)) ## Error #> Error in g(x = 1, y = 1) : `x` must not be equal to `y`. try(g(x = 1, y = 2)) ## No error

title: Check Formula Argument — arg_formula
text: Checks whether argument formula.

title: Check Formula Argument — arg_formula
code: arg_formula(x, one_sided = NULL, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Formula Argument — arg_formula
text: x argument checked one_sided NULL logical; TRUE, checks x formula one side (right side); FALSE, checks x formula sides; NULL (default), checks x formula. .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Formula Argument — arg_formula
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Formula Argument — arg_formula
code: form1 <- ~a + b form2 <- y ~ a + b not_form <- 1:3  try(arg_formula(form1))    # No error try(arg_formula(form2))    # No error try(arg_formula(not_form)) # Error: not a formula #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `not_form` must be a formula.  try(arg_formula(form1,                 one_sided = TRUE)) # No error #> Error in arg_formula(form1, one_sided = TRUE) :  #>   `one_sided` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(arg_formula(form2,                 one_sided = TRUE)) # Error, not one-sided #> Error in arg_formula(form2, one_sided = TRUE) :  #>   `one_sided` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE).  try(arg_formula(form1,                 one_sided = FALSE)) # Error, only one-sided #> Error in arg_formula(form1, one_sided = FALSE) :  #>   `one_sided` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(arg_formula(form2,                 one_sided = FALSE)) # No error #> Error in arg_formula(form2, one_sided = FALSE) :  #>   `one_sided` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE).

title: Check Function Argument — arg_function

title: Check Function Argument — arg_function
code: arg_function(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Function Argument — arg_function
text: x argument checked .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Function Argument — arg_function
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Function Argument — arg_function
code: f <- function(z) {   arg_function(z) }  try(f(print))   # No error try(f("print")) # Error: must be a function #> Error in f("print") : `z` must be a function.

title: Check Index Argument — arg_index
text: Checks whether argument valid column index (arg_index()) data set vector thereof (arg_indices()).

title: Check Index Argument — arg_index
code: arg_index(   x,   data,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .arg_data = rlang::caller_arg(data),   .msg = NULL )  arg_indices(   x,   data,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .arg_data = rlang::caller_arg(data),   .msg = NULL )

title: Check Index Argument — arg_index
text: x argument checked data data set (.e., matrix data frame) .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .arg_data name argument supplied data appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Index Argument — arg_index
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Index Argument — arg_index
text: arg_indices(), error thrown unless one following true: x vector counts (see arg_counts()) less equal ncol(data) x character vector values subset colnames(data) arg_index(), x additionally must length equal 1. Passing arg_index() ensures data[, x] (data matrix) data[[x]] (x data frame) evaluate correctly. data column names, error thrown x character vector.

title: Check Index Argument — arg_index
code: dat <- data.frame(col1 = 1:5,                   col2 = 6:10)  f <- function(z) {   arg_index(z, dat) }  try(f(1))         # No error try(f(3))         # Error: not a valid index #> Error in f(3) :  #>   `z` must be the name or index of a column in `dat`. try(f("col1"))    # No error try(f("bad_col")) # Error: not a valid index #> Error in f("bad_col") :  #>   `z` must be the name or index of a column in `dat`. try(f(1:2))       # Error: arg_index() requires scalar #> Error in f(1:2) :  #>   `z` must be the name or index of a column in `dat`.  mat <- matrix(1:9, ncol = 3)  g <- function(z) {   arg_indices(z, mat) }  try(g(1))     # No error try(g(1:3))   # No error try(g("col")) # Error: `mat` has no names #> Error in g("col") : `z` must be the index of a column in `mat`.

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
text: Checks whether argument specified class (arg_is()) specified class (arg_is_not()).

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
code: arg_is(x, class, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_is_not(x, class, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
text: x argument checked class character vector one classes check x . .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
text: arg_is() arg_is_not() use inherits() test class membership. arg_is() throws error elements class(x) class. arg_is_not() throws error elements class(x) class. Sometimes can permissive; combining arg_is() arg_and() can useful requiring object multiple classes. Alternatively, combining arg_is_not() arg_or() can useful requiring object specific combination classes. See Examples.

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
code: obj <- structure(list(1),                  class = "test")  try(arg_is(obj, "test"))            # No error try(arg_is(obj, c("test", "quiz"))) # No error try(arg_is(obj, "quiz"))            # Error #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `obj` must inherit from class <quiz>.  try(arg_is_not(obj, "test"))            # Error #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `obj` must not inherit from class <test>. try(arg_is_not(obj, c("test", "quiz"))) # Error #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `obj` must not inherit from class <test/quiz>. try(arg_is_not(obj, "quiz"))            # No error  # Multiple classes obj2 <- structure(list(1),                   class = c("test", "essay"))  try(arg_is(obj2, c("test", "quiz")))     # No error try(arg_is_not(obj2, c("test", "quiz"))) # Error #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `obj2` must not inherit from class <test/quiz>.  ## Require argument to be of multiple classes try(arg_and(obj2,             arg_is("test"),             arg_is("quiz"))) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `obj2` must inherit from class <quiz>.  ## Require argument to not be a specific combination of ## multiple classes try(arg_or(obj2,            arg_is_not("test"),            arg_is_not("essay"))) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `obj2` must not inherit from class <test> or must not inherit from class #> <essay>.

title: Check Argument Length — arg_length
text: Checks whether argument specified length.

title: Check Argument Length — arg_length
code: arg_length(x, len = 1L, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Argument Length — arg_length
text: x argument checked len integer; allowed length(s) x. Default 1. .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Argument Length — arg_length
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Argument Length — arg_length
text: len can contain multiple allowed counts; error thrown length(x) equal value len.

title: Check Argument Length — arg_length
code: obj <- 1:4  try(arg_length(obj, 1)) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `obj` must have length 1. try(arg_length(obj, 4)) try(arg_length(obj, c(1, 4)))  # These test the same thing: try(arg_length(obj, c(0:3))) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `obj` must have length 0, 1, 2, or 3. try(when_not_null(obj,                   arg_length(1:3))) #> Error in tryCatchOne(expr, names, parentenv, handlers[[1L]]) :  #>   When not NULL, `obj` must have length 1, 2, or 3.

title: Check Logical Argument — arg_logical
text: Checks whether argument logical vector (arg_logical()) logical scalar (arg_flag()), .e., single logical value. Logical values include TRUE FALSE.

title: Check Logical Argument — arg_logical
code: arg_logical(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_flag(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Logical Argument — arg_logical
text: x argument checked .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Logical Argument — arg_logical
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Logical Argument — arg_logical
text: NA values arg_flag() cause error thrown.

title: Check Logical Argument — arg_logical
code: obj <- TRUE  try(arg_flag(obj))    # No error #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `obj` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(arg_logical(obj)) # No error  obj <- c(TRUE, FALSE)  try(arg_flag(obj))    # Error: must be a scalar #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `obj` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(arg_logical(obj)) # No error  obj <- 1L  try(arg_flag(obj))    # Error must be logical #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `obj` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(arg_logical(obj)) # Error must be logical #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `obj` must be a logical vector.

title: Check Named Argument — arg_named
text: Checks whether argument valid (non-NULL, non-empty, non-NA) names.

title: Check Named Argument — arg_named
code: arg_named(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_colnamed(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Named Argument — arg_named
text: x argument checked .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Named Argument — arg_named
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Named Argument — arg_named
text: arg_named() works vectors, lists, data frames. check whether matrix column names, use arg_colnamed() (also works data frames, vectors lists).

title: Check Named Argument — arg_named
code: obj <- c(1,          B = 2,          C = 3)  try(arg_named(obj)) # Error: one name is blank #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `obj` must have non-empty and non-NA names.  names(obj)[1L] <- "A" try(arg_named(obj)) # No error  obj2 <- unname(obj) try(arg_named(obj2)) # Error: no names #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `obj2` must have non-empty and non-NA names.  # Matrix and data frame mat <- matrix(1:6, ncol = 2L) colnames(mat) <- c("A", "B")  try(arg_named(mat))    # Error: matrices are not named #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `mat` must have non-empty and non-NA names. try(arg_colnamed(mat)) # No error  dat <- as.data.frame(mat) try(arg_named(dat))    # No error: data frames are named try(arg_colnamed(dat)) # No error  colnames(mat) <- NULL try(arg_colnamed(mat)) # Error: no colnames #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `mat` must have non-empty and non-NA names.

title: Check NA in Argument — arg_no_NA
text: Checks whether argument contain NA values (arg_no_NA()), contains NA values (arg_all_NA()), scalar NA (arg_is_NA()).

title: Check NA in Argument — arg_no_NA
code: arg_no_NA(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_is_NA(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_all_NA(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check NA in Argument — arg_no_NA
text: x argument checked .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check NA in Argument — arg_no_NA
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check NA in Argument — arg_no_NA
text: arg_no_NA() throws error anyNA(x) 0. arg_all_NA() throws error (.na(x)) FALSE. arg_is_NA() throws error length(x) 1 anyNA(x) FALSE. arg_no_NA() useful checking meaningful argument supplied. arg_all_NA() arg_is_NA() primarily used arg_or() denote NA allowed argument.

title: Check NA in Argument — arg_no_NA
code: f <- function(x) {   arg_no_NA(x)  ## x must not be NA }  try(f(1))           ## No error try(f(NA))          ## Error: x is NA #> Error in f(NA) : `x` must not be NA. try(f(c(1, NA, 3))) ## Error: x contains NA #> Error in f(c(1, NA, 3)) : `x` must not contain NA values.  f2 <- function(y) {   arg_all_NA(y) ## y must be NA }  try(f2(NA))          ## No error try(f2(c(NA, NA)))   ## No error try(f2(1))           ## Error: y is not NA #> Error in f2(1) : `y` must be NA. try(f2(c(1, NA, 3))) ## Error: y is not all NA #> Error in f2(c(1, NA, 3)) :  #>   `y` must only contain NA values.

title: Check NULL Argument — arg_non_null
text: Checks whether argument non-NULL (arg_non_null()) NULL (arg_null()). arg_non_null() throws error length(x) 0. arg_null() throws error length(x) 0.

title: Check NULL Argument — arg_non_null
code: arg_non_null(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_null(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check NULL Argument — arg_non_null
text: x argument checked .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check NULL Argument — arg_non_null
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check NULL Argument — arg_non_null
text: , NULL refers length-0 object, including NULL, logical(0L), list(), 3[FALSE], etc. arg_non_null() useful checking meaningful argument supplied. arg_null() primarily used arg_or() denote NULL allowed argument.

title: Check NULL Argument — arg_non_null
code: f <- function(x = NULL, y = NULL) {   arg_non_null(x) ## x must not be NULL   arg_null(y)     ## y must be NULL }  try(f(x = 1,    y = NULL)) ## No error try(f(x = NULL, y = NULL)) ## Error: x is NULL #> Error in f(x = NULL, y = NULL) : `x` must be non-NULL. try(f(x = 1,    y = 1))    ## Error: y is non-NULL #> Error in f(x = 1, y = 1) : `y` must be NULL.  # Any object of length 0 is considered NULL try(f(x = numeric())) ## Error: x is NULL #> Error in f(x = numeric()) : `x` must be non-NULL. try(f(x = list()))    ## Error: x is NULL #> Error in f(x = list()) : `x` must be non-NULL.  test <- c(1, 2)[c(FALSE, FALSE)] try(f(x = test))      ## Error: x is NULL #> Error in f(x = test) : `x` must be non-NULL.  # arg_null() is best used in and_or(): f2 <- function(z) {   arg_or(z,          arg_null(),          arg_number()) }  try(f2(NULL)) ## No error; z can be NULL try(f2(1))    ## No error; z can be a number try(f2(TRUE)) ## Error: z must be NULL or a number #> Error in f2(TRUE) : `z` must be NULL or a number.

title: Check Numeric Argument — arg_numeric
text: Checks whether argument numeric, additional constraints desired. arg_numeric() simply checks whether argument numeric. arg_number() checks whether argument numeric scalar (.e., single number). arg_whole_numeric() arg_whole_number() check whether argument whole numeric vector scalar, respectively. arg_counts() arg_count() check whether argument non-negative whole numeric vector scalar, respectively.

title: Check Numeric Argument — arg_numeric
code: arg_numeric(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_number(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_whole_numeric(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_whole_number(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_counts(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_count(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Numeric Argument — arg_numeric
text: x argument checked .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Numeric Argument — arg_numeric
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Numeric Argument — arg_numeric
text: whole number decided testing whether value integer (.e., using .integer()) abs(x - trunc(x)) < sqrt(.Machine$double.eps). tolerance used .equal() compare values.

title: Check Numeric Argument — arg_numeric
code: count <- 3 whole <- -4 num <- .943  try(arg_number(count))       # No error try(arg_whole_number(count)) # No error try(arg_count(count))        # No error  try(arg_number(whole))       # No error try(arg_whole_number(whole)) # No error try(arg_count(whole))        # Error: negatives not allowed #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `whole` must be a count (a non-negative whole number).  try(arg_number(num))       # No error try(arg_whole_number(num)) # Error: not a whole number #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `num` must be a whole number. try(arg_count(num))        # Error: not a count #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `num` must be a count (a non-negative whole number).  nums <- c(0, .5, 1)  try(arg_number(nums))  # Error: not a single number #> Error in eval(expr, envir) : `nums` must be a single number. try(arg_numeric(nums)) # No error try(arg_counts(nums))  # Error: not counts #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `nums` must be a vector of counts (non-negative whole numeric values).

what: Check That Arguments Meet Some or All Criteria — arg_or
title: Check That Arguments Meet Some or All Criteria — arg_or
text: arg_or() checks whether argument meets least one given criterion. arg_and() checks whether argument meets given criteria.

title: Check That Arguments Meet Some or All Criteria — arg_or
code: arg_or(x, ..., .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)  arg_and(x, ..., .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check That Arguments Meet Some or All Criteria — arg_or
text: x argument checked ... arg_*() functions unevaluated function calls applied x. See Examples. .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check That Arguments Meet Some or All Criteria — arg_or
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check That Arguments Meet Some or All Criteria — arg_or
text: arg_or(), error thrown x fails supplied checks. can useful argument can take one several input types. arg_and(), error thrown x fails supplied checks. less useful , checks can simply occur sequence without arg_and(), arg_and() can supplied ... argument arg_or() create complicated criteria. ... arguments can passed either functions, e.g.,   unevaluated function calls x argument absent, e.g.,   mixture . functions best provide clean error message composed error messages failed checks. many options, can yield complicated error message, use caution. arg_and() marks check (✔) passed checks x (✖) failed checks.
code: arg_or(x,        arg_number,        arg_string,        arg_flag) arg_or(x,        arg_number(),        arg_string(),        arg_flag())

title: Check That Arguments Meet Some or All Criteria — arg_or
code: # `arg_or()` f <- function(z) {   arg_or(z,          arg_number,          arg_string,          arg_flag) }  try(f(1))      # No error try(f("test")) # No error try(f(TRUE))   # No error #> Error in f(TRUE) :  #>   `z` must be a number, a string, or a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(f(1:4))    # Error: neither a number, string, #> Error in f(1:4) :  #>   `z` must be a single number, a string, or a logical value (TRUE or #> FALSE). #              #        or flag, but a vector  # `arg_and()` g <- function(z) {   arg_and(z,           arg_counts,           arg_length(len = 2),           arg_lt(bound = 5)) }  try(g(c(1, 2)))     # No error try(g(c(1, 7)))     # Error: not < 5 #> Error in g(c(1, 7)) : All of the following conditions must be met: #> ✔ `z` must be a vector of counts (non-negative whole numeric values) #> ✔ `z` must have length 2 #> ✖ each element of `z` must be less than 5 try(g(c(1.1, 2.1))) # Error: not counts #> Error in g(c(1.1, 2.1)) :  #>   All of the following conditions must be met: #> ✖ `z` must be a vector of counts (non-negative whole numeric values) #> ✔ `z` must have length 2 #> ✔ each element of `z` must be less than 5 try(g(4))           # Error: not length 2 #> Error in g(4) : All of the following conditions must be met: #> ✔ `z` must be a vector of counts (non-negative whole numeric values) #> ✖ `z` must have length 2 #> ✔ `z` must be less than 5 try(g("bad"))       # Error: no criteria satisfied #> Error in g("bad") : All of the following conditions must be met: #> ✖ `z` must be a vector of counts (non-negative whole numeric values) #> ✖ `z` must have length 2 #> ✖ `z` must be less than 5  # Chaining together `arg_and()` and `arg_or()` h <- function(z) {   arg_or(z,          arg_all_NA,          arg_and(arg_count,                  arg_lt(5)),          arg_and(arg_string,                  arg_element(c("a", "b", "c")))) }  try(h(NA))  # No error try(h(1))   # No error try(h("a")) # No error try(h(7))   # Error: not < 5 #> Error in h(7) :  #>   At least one of the following conditions must be met: #> • `z` must be NA #> • `z` must be a count (a non-negative whole number) and less than 5 #> • `z` must be a string and one of "a", "b", or "c" try(h("d")) # Error: not in "a", "b", or "c" #> Error in h("d") :  #>   At least one of the following conditions must be met: #> • `z` must be NA #> • `z` must be a count (a non-negative whole number) and less than 5 #> • `z` must be a string and one of "a", "b", or "c"

title: Check Supplied Argument — arg_supplied

code: arg_supplied(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Supplied Argument — arg_supplied
text: x argument checked .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Supplied Argument — arg_supplied
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Supplied Argument — arg_supplied
code: f <- function(z) {   arg_supplied(z) }  try(f(1)) ## No error: argument supplied try(f())  ## Error! #> Error in f() : An argument to `z` must be supplied.  # Will not throw for NULL or default arguments try(f(NULL)) ## No error: argument supplied  f2 <- function(z = NULL) {   arg_supplied(z) }  try(f2()) ## No error; default provided

title: Check Symmetric Matrix Argument — arg_symmetric
text: arg_symmetric() checks whether argument square, symmetric, numeric matrix. arg_cov() checks whether argument like covariance matrix (.e., square symmetric non-negative diagonal entries). arg_cor() checks whether argument like correlation matrix (.e., square symmetric values -1 1 ones diagonal). arg_distance() checks whether argument like distance matrix (.e., square symmetric non-negative entries zeros diagonal).

title: Check Symmetric Matrix Argument — arg_symmetric
code: arg_symmetric(   x,   tol = 100 * .Machine$double.eps,   ...,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .msg = NULL )  arg_cov(   x,   tol = 100 * .Machine$double.eps,   ...,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .msg = NULL )  arg_cor(   x,   tol = 100 * .Machine$double.eps,   ...,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .msg = NULL )  arg_distance(   x,   tol = 100 * .Machine$double.eps,   ...,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .msg = NULL )

title: Check Symmetric Matrix Argument — arg_symmetric
text: x argument checked tol numeric; tolerance value used assess symmetry numerical bounds. Passed isSymmetric(). ... arguments passed isSymmetric() (eventually .equal()). .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Symmetric Matrix Argument — arg_symmetric
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Symmetric Matrix Argument — arg_symmetric
text: functions check argument square, symmetric, numeric matrix. can useful function can accept covariance matrix, correlation matrix, distance matrix. arg_cov() throw error values diagonal less -tol. arg_cor() throw error values greater 1 + tol absolute value diagonal entries -1 - tol 1 + tol. arg_distance() thrown error values less -tol diagonal entries -tol tol. tolerance just slightly greater 0 allow numeric imprecision. checks semi-definiteness rank performed.

title: Check Symmetric Matrix Argument — arg_symmetric
code: set.seed(1234) mat <- matrix(rnorm(12), ncol = 3L) # Non square sym_mat <- -crossprod(mat)          # Square, symmetric cov_mat <- cov(mat)                 # Covariance cor_mat <- cor(mat)                 # Correlation dist_mat <- as.matrix(dist(mat))    # Distance  try(arg_symmetric(mat))     # Error: not square #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `mat` must be a square, symmetric, numeric matrix. try(arg_symmetric(sym_mat)) # No error  try(arg_cov(sym_mat)) # Error: diagonal must be non-negative #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `sym_mat` must be a square, symmetric matrix with non-negative values on #> the diagonal. try(arg_cov(cov_mat)) # No error  try(arg_cor(cov_mat)) # Error: values must be in [-1, 1] #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   All values in `cov_mat` must be between -1 and 1. try(arg_cor(cor_mat)) # No error  try(arg_distance(cor_mat))  # Error: diagonal must be 0 #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   `cor_mat` must be a square, symmetric matrix with zeros on the diagonal. try(arg_distance(dist_mat)) # No error

title: Check Unique Argument — arg_unique

title: Check Unique Argument — arg_unique
code: arg_unique(x, ..., .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL)

title: Check Unique Argument — arg_unique
text: x argument checked ... argument passed anyDuplicated(), performs checking. .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message.

title: Check Unique Argument — arg_unique
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Unique Argument — arg_unique
code: f <- function(z) {   arg_unique(z) }  try(f(1:3))     # No error try(f(NULL))    # No error for NULL try(f(c(1, 1))) # Error: repeated values #> Error in f(c(1, 1)) : `z` must only contain unique values.

what: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
title: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
text: functions similar stop()/cli::cli_abort(), warning()/cli::cli_warn(), message()/cli::cli_inform(), throwing error, warning, message, respectively. Minor processing done capitalize first letter message, add period end (makes sense ), add information calling function.

title: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
code: err(m, call = rlang::caller_env(2), .envir = rlang::caller_env())  wrn(m, immediate = TRUE, .envir = rlang::caller_env())  msg(m, .envir = rlang::caller_env())

title: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
text: m message displayed, passed message argument rlang::abort(), rlang::warn(), rlang::inform(). call execution environment function. corresponding function call retrieved mentioned error messages source error. See rlang::abort() details. .envir environment evaluate glue expressions . See rlang::abort() details. Typically need changed. immediate whether output warning immediately (TRUE, default) save warnings end execution (FALSE). See warning() details. Note default differs warning().

title: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
text: functions simple wrappers corresponding functions rlang, namely rlang::abort() err(), rlang::warn() wrn(), rlang::inform() msg(), function almost identically cli versions. main differences additionally process input (capitalizing first character message adding period end needed, unless multiple strings provided). err() used inside arg_*() functions arg.

title: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
code: f <- function(x) {   err("this is an error, and {.arg x} is {.type {x}}") }  try(f(1)) #> Error in eval(expr, envir) :  #>   This is an error, and `x` is a number.  g <- function(x) {   wrn("this warning displayed last", immediate = FALSE)   wrn("this warning displayed first") }  try(g(1)) #> Warning: This warning displayed last. #> Warning: This warning displayed first.  h <- function() {   msg("is a period added at the end?")   msg("not when the message ends in punctuation!")   msg(c("or when multiple",         "!" = "messages",         "v" = "are",         "*" = "displayed"))   msg("otherwise yes") }  h() #> Is a period added at the end? #> Not when the message ends in punctuation! #> or when multiple #> ! messages #> ✔ are #> • displayed #> Otherwise yes.

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
text: alternative match.arg() improved error messages via cli. Returns first choice x NULL, supports partial matching.

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
code: match_arg(   x,   choices,   several.ok = FALSE,   ignore.case = FALSE,   .context = NULL,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x) )

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
text: x string (character vector several.ok = TRUE) match choices. NULL, first element choices returned. choices character vector valid values. several.ok logical; TRUE, x may contain one element. Defaults FALSE. ignore.case logical; FALSE (default), matching case sensitive, TRUE, case ignored. .context optional character string providing context error messages (e.g., function object name). Prepended error message supplied evaluated cli. .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied.

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
text: character string (vector, several.ok = TRUE) matched element(s) choices. match, error thrown. several.okay = TRUE, error thrown least one match.

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
text: Partial matching supported via pmatch(). match found, error thrown listing valid choices. Checks run x prior matching: first, arg_supplied() used check whether x supplied; arg_string() (several.ok = TRUE) arg_character() (several.ok = FALSE) used check whether x valid string character vector, respectively.

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
code: f <- function(z = NULL) {   match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"),             ignore.case = TRUE) }  try(f())            # "None" (first choice returned) #> [1] "None" try(f("partial"))   # "Partial" #> Error in match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"), ignore.case = TRUE) :  #>   `several.ok` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(f("p"))         # "Partial" (partial match) #> Error in match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"), ignore.case = TRUE) :  #>   `several.ok` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(f(c("e", "p"))) # Error: several.ok = FALSE #> Error in match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"), ignore.case = TRUE) :  #>   `several.ok` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE).  # several.ok = TRUE g <- function(z = NULL) {   match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"),             several.ok = TRUE) }  try(g("exact"))               # Error: case not ignored #> Error in match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"), several.ok = TRUE) :  #>   `several.ok` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(g("Exact"))               # "Exact" #> Error in match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"), several.ok = TRUE) :  #>   `several.ok` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(g(c("Exact", "Partial"))) # "Exact", "Partial" #> Error in match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"), several.ok = TRUE) :  #>   `several.ok` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(g(c("Exact", "Wrong")))   # "Exact" #> Error in match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"), several.ok = TRUE) :  #>   `several.ok` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE). try(g(c("Wrong1", "Wrong2"))) # Error: no match #> Error in match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"), several.ok = TRUE) :  #>   `several.ok` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE).  h <- function(z = NULL) {   match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"),             .context = "in {.fun h},") }  try(h("Wrong")) # Error with context #> Error in match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"), .context = "in {.fun h},") :  #>   `several.ok` must be a logical value (TRUE or FALSE).

title: Check Arguments When Supplied — when_supplied

title: Check Arguments When Supplied — when_supplied
code: when_supplied(x, ..., .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x))  when_not_null(x, ..., .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x))

title: Check Arguments When Supplied — when_supplied
text: x argument checked ... arg_*() functions unevaluated function calls applied x. See Examples. .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied.

title: Check Arguments When Supplied — when_supplied
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Arguments When Supplied — when_supplied
text: error thrown x supplied fails one supplied checks (when_supplied()) NULL fails one supplied checks (when_not_null()). ... arguments can passed either functions, e.g.,   unevaluated function calls x argument absent, e.g.,   mixture . when_supplied() makes sense use argument default value can omitted. when_not_null() makes sense use argument default value NULL.
code: when_supplied(x,               arg_number,               arg_gt) when_supplied(x,               arg_number(),               arg_gt(bound = 0))

title: Check Arguments When Supplied — when_supplied
code: f <- function(z) {   when_supplied(z,                 arg_number,                 arg_between(c(0, 1))) }  try(f())    # No error: not supplied try(f("a")) # Error: not a number #> Error in tryCatchOne(expr, names, parentenv, handlers[[1L]]) :  #>   When supplied, `z` must be a number. try(f(2))   # Error: not within 0-1 range #> Error in tryCatchOne(expr, names, parentenv, handlers[[1L]]) :  #>   When supplied, `z` must be between 0 and 1 (inclusive). try(f(.7))  # No error: number within range  g <- function(z = NULL) {   when_not_null(z,                 arg_number,                 arg_between(c(0, 1))) }  try(g())     # No error: NULL okay try(g(NULL)) # No error: NULL okay try(g("a"))  # Error: not a number #> Error in tryCatchOne(expr, names, parentenv, handlers[[1L]]) :  #>   When not NULL, `z` must be a number. try(g(2))    # Error: not within 0-1 range #> Error in tryCatchOne(expr, names, parentenv, handlers[[1L]]) :  #>   When not NULL, `z` must be between 0 and 1 (inclusive). try(g(.7))   # No error: number within range
